sections.heading: Molecular pathogenesis bifurcation
sections.content: The molecular pathogenesis of Merkel cell carcinoma (MCC) is largely bifurcated based on the presence or absence of Merkel cell polyomavirus (MCPyV).

sections.heading: Virus-positive MCC
sections.content: In virus-positive MCC, which comprises 43% to 100% of cases, the clonal integration of MCPyV into the host genome is an early oncogenic event. The virus expresses truncated forms of the Large T antigen and small T antigen. These oncoproteins drive proliferation by inactivating key tumor suppressor proteins: the Large T antigen binds to and inhibits retinoblastoma protein (pRb), while both antigens can interfere with p53 pathway function. This viral-driven model results in a tumor with a relatively low mutational burden.

sections.heading: Virus-negative MCC
sections.content: In contrast, virus-negative MCC arises through a UV radiation-dependent pathway, evidenced by a high mutational burden and a characteristic UV signature (cytosine to thymine transitions). Genetic analyses of these tumors frequently show mutations in tumor suppressor genes, including TP53 and RB1. Additionally, alterations in other cancer-associated genes have been reported.

sections.heading: Germline mutations in early-onset cases
sections.content: Genomic studies have noted that approximately 20% of patients under 50 years of age with MCC may harbor germline mutations in known cancer-associated genes, suggesting a potential heritable component in early-onset cases.

sections.heading: Biological heterogeneity and immunotherapy
sections.content: The distinct molecular pathways contribute to the biological heterogeneity of MCC, with virus-negative tumors being more heavily mutated and potentially more susceptible to immunotherapies like PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors. The high expression of programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) in some MCC tumors, regardless of viral status, provides a rationale for the efficacy of checkpoint inhibitor therapy.
